role_summary: SNCC field worker in the Southwest Georgia Project (the SNCC organizing campaign led by Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod, catalog entry 18, that anchored the Albany Movement); participant in Worth County, Georgia mass meetings; SNCC bookkeeper, voter registrar, and welfare-services-information community organizer.
ai_reading: McClary's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 36), with Charles McLaurin (entry 17) as the top match and the broader cluster including Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96), Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92), and Robert L. Carter (entry 108). The novel observation his page surfaces is the Southwest-Georgia-Project signature: McClary worked the same Sherrod-led SNCC field network that anchored the Albany Movement, and the embedding catches that organizing-tradition register through his testimony as continuous with the broader Mississippi-and-Alabama SNCC voter-registration cohort. His relatively low neighbor counts (peak 36 versus the catalog median of around 100-200) suggest a shorter or more topically-focused interview transcript, consistent with the LoC description of his interview as a "short interview."
biographical_paragraph: While McClary's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), the external biographical record of the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project is documented in Rev. Charles Sherrod's catalog entry 18 (the Sherrod-led project's founding director) [src: 1, src: 2]. McClary's oral history was conducted by Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany, Georgia in March 2013; in it he describes his involvement in the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project alongside Sherrod and other Albany Movement organizers across the early-to-mid 1960s. He attended mass meetings in Worth County (the rural Georgia county that was one of the project's organizing focuses), kept the books for the SNCC project budget, registered voters across the county, and worked to inform Black residents about the welfare services available to them [src: 1].
